Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Machine Minds

Warren McCulloch

1898 – 1969

Cybernetic neural form, embodiment, and mind without a ghostly homunculus.

Warren S. McCulloch should not be read merely as a precursor of artificial neural networks, nor merely as a mid-century “mind-as-computer” mechanist. His mature project is better described as experimental epistemology: an attempt to explain how finite organisms can know, intend, remember, choose, err, and become conscious, using the resources of neurophysiology, logic, information theory, cybernetics, and a deliberately chastened metaphysics. In that project, the theological language is not incidental ornament. It marks the limits of mechanism, the misuse of God as a pseudo-explanation, the faith presupposed by empiricism, and the moral danger of claiming divine epistemic finality.

The philosophy of mind that can be extracted from McCulloch is a finite cybernetic monism: mind is not an immaterial substance added to matter, but neither is it reducible to brute matter conceived as inert stuff. Mind consists in ideas and purposes; ideas are invariants, regularities, or information; purposes are feedback-governed functions or ends-in-operation. Consciousness is not a single occult essence. It is at least three things: forensic reportability, higher-order “ideas of ideas,” and self-reference in which the knower becomes one object among the things known. The “soul,” where he retains the word, is not a self-sufficient Cartesian or scholastic substance with a unique power of perception; it is, at most, the private and mostly incommunicable interiority of an information-processing organism whose efferent channels can express only a minute fraction of what is occurring within it. McCulloch’s own term for the resulting metaphysical direction is “indifferent monism.” He thinks organisms, including brains, are machines, but “machine” here means a finite, lawful, information-transforming, feedback-governed system, not a trivial clockwork automaton.

1. Why theology matters in McCulloch

McCulloch’s religious and theological background is not biographically decorative. In “What Is a Number, that a Man May Know It, and a Man, that He May Know a Number?” he says he came from a family that produced theologians, was himself destined for the ministry, and grew up around figures such as Henry Sloan Coffin, Harry Emerson Fosdick, and the Quaker philosopher Rufus Jones. He recounts telling Jones that the question he wanted to answer was what a number is, that a human can know it, and what a human is, that he can know number; Jones replied that McCulloch would be busy with that for life. McCulloch presents the story not as anecdote but as the genesis of his entire intellectual vocation.

That vocation became “experimental epistemology.” In the same lecture, McCulloch says his interest had always been to reduce epistemology to an experimental science, and he defines the task as giving a satisfactory account of how we know what we know in terms of the physics, chemistry, anatomy, and physiology of the biological system. This is not simply psychology, not simply physiology, and not simply philosophy. It is a physiological account of knowledge, where the traditional questions of epistemology are forced into the laboratory without losing their metaphysical seriousness.

His theology is partly negative: he thinks invoking God, Nature, Vital Force, Nervous Energy, or the Unconscious as a name for what one does not understand is both futile and profane. In “Mysterium Iniquitatis of Sinful Man Aspiring into the Place of God,” he explicitly says that instead of explaining the unknown by the unknown, one should seek mechanisms; a machine that performs the function to be explained removes the apparent warrant for miracle. This is not a casual atheistic jab. It is a theological discipline: do not make God a placeholder for ignorance, because that corrupts both science and theology.

At the same time, McCulloch’s theology is partly positive. He repeatedly returns to the claim that empiricism itself rests on faith: the truth of every primary sensory proposition cannot be tested, so the empiricist, like the Thomist, must believe that the senses were not given in order to deceive. His final warning in “Mysterium Iniquitatis” is that we remain safe only if we remember that belief in the senses is an act of faith, that brains corrupt rather than generate information, and that our best hypotheses are guesses open to refutation. The sin in the title is therefore epistemic as much as moral: the arrogance of finite man pretending to occupy the place of God by claiming ultimate universal truth or knowledge of the future.

2. Mind and body: ideas, purposes, stuff, process

McCulloch’s most compressed definition appears in “Why the Mind Is in the Head”: “mind” means ideas and purposes; “body” means stuff and process. But the definition is more subtle than it first appears. He does not say ideas are ghostly substances; he says ideas are regularities or invariants in the flux of events. A theorem, a sensed quality, a perceived object, and a mathematical abstraction are all “ideas” insofar as each is an invariant under transformations. Ideas are not “material” or “efficient” causes in the physicist’s causal chain; they are forms, orders, or informational structures embodied in processes but not identical with mass-energy as such.

This is the first key to his philosophy of mind: McCulloch rejects both crude materialism and substance dualism. Against crude materialism, he insists that ideas and purposes are real objects of discourse, indispensable to understanding mind. Against dualism, he insists that these ideas and purposes can be embodied in finite physical circuitry. Mind is not outside the world; it is the world’s formal and purposive organization as realized in nervous systems and, in principle, machines. This is why he can say that the physicist who accounts for his own theoretical activity must become a neurophysiologist and, in doing so, is accused of becoming a metaphysician.

His concept of purpose comes from cybernetics. A negative-feedback circuit returns information about the consequence of an act and uses that information to correct deviation from a state. The state toward which the system returns is its goal, aim, or end; this is what he means by function. Purpose is therefore not an occult final cause imposed from outside mechanism. It is an operational structure: an error-correcting loop in which information, rather than energy, is what must return.

This allows McCulloch to rehabilitate teleology without reintroducing vitalism. A guided missile, an eye movement reflex, homeostasis, appetitive behavior, ethical cooperation, and theoretical inquiry can all be analyzed as feedback-governed purposive systems. His move is philosophically important because it separates finality from supernaturalism. A purpose is not a miracle. It is a circuit architecture.

3. The logical neuron and the computational embodiment of mind

The 1943 McCulloch-Pitts paper, “A Logical Calculus of the Ideas Immanent in Nervous Activity,” is the formal basis of his philosophy of mind. Its abstract states the crucial claim: because nervous activity is all-or-none, neural events and their relations can be treated by propositional logic; the behavior of every net can be described in these terms, and for any suitable logical expression one can find a net that behaves as the expression describes. The point is not merely that neurons resemble logic gates. The point is that “ideas” can be immanent in nervous activity because nervous activity can realize logical structure.

McCulloch later gives the philosophical version of this doctrine in “Why the Mind Is in the Head.” A nervous impulse is an atomic event and also a signal; it is true if what it proposes is true and false if it arises from the wrong cause. Nervous impulses are therefore “atomic propositions on the move,” to which a temporally indexed propositional calculus applies. Nets without circles can realize logical consequences of inputs; nets with circles introduce existential operators for the past, because a reverberating circuit indicates that something occurred at some time without preserving the exact time.

The theory of memory follows from this. A reverberating memory retains the form of a happening while losing its date; the “such-and-such” is wrenched out of time. McCulloch calls this an eternal idea in a transitory memory. This is a remarkable phrase: the eternity of the idea is not Platonic independence from the body, but invariance of form despite temporal loss. Enduring memory, however, cannot be only reverberation; he later argues that lasting memory is pinned to the brain because brain injuries, not injuries elsewhere, cause memory loss, and he suspects enduring memory involves growth or change at neuronal junctions.

The machine claim must be handled carefully. McCulloch does not say that every actual mental process had already been explained by actual neural mechanisms. He says that if one can specify in a finite and unambiguous way what a brain does with information, then a machine can be designed to do it; Pitts and McCulloch had proved this constructively. John von Neumann, in discussion after “Why the Mind Is in the Head,” correctly described McCulloch’s method as giving sufficient mechanisms or proofs of possibility, not necessarily claiming that the proposed mechanisms are the ones the brain actually uses.

This distinction matters philosophically. McCulloch’s machine philosophy is not naive identity theory. It is an existence-proof strategy: once a finite circuit can perform the relevant function, one has removed the need to posit a miracle or ghostly faculty for that function. The actual neurophysiology may still be unknown, and McCulloch repeatedly admits that many particular cortical hypotheses are likely wrong.

4. Ideas as invariants: universals without Platonic separation

McCulloch’s account of universals is one of the deepest parts of his philosophy of mind. The problem is ancient: how can a finite embodied knower recognize “the same” form across many variable appearances? How can the mind know triangle, chord, number, word, face, or object when every sensation is particular?

His answer is invariance. In “Why the Mind Is in the Head,” he says we cannot know what to look for in a brain when it has an idea except that the process must be invariant under all the conditions in which that brain has that idea. The general theory of securing ideas is “little more” than an application of group theory: for any input figure, it is possible to calculate an output invariant under a group of transformations. To have an idea is, in the most general cybernetic sense, to stabilize an output under transformations of the input.

This is why McCulloch and Pitts’s 1947 work on universals matters. In that work, they modeled mechanisms by which auditory and visual forms could be recognized independently of perturbations. The Springer abstract summarizes the two mechanisms as one that averages over a group and another that reduces the apparition to a standard; these are extensions of their account of knowing universals.

The philosophical consequence is that universals are neither floating Platonic entities nor mere names. They are operational invariants embodied in finite circuits. A form is universal for a system when the system’s output remains stable across a defined class of transformations. This gives a neurocybernetic interpretation of the medieval and early modern problem of universals: the universal is a functional invariance relation secured by a physical system.

McCulloch’s account of number is parallel. In the Korzybski lecture, he adopts Russell’s definition of number as a class of classes that can be put into one-to-one correspondence. But he distinguishes small whole numbers, which he takes to be perceptibles shared with animals, from larger integers, which require counting practices, symbolic conventions, communication, and logos. The first half of the question “what is a number?” is answered by logical definition plus embodied perceptual capacities; the second half, “what is man?” requires a logic of relations and propositions adequate to the knower.

This yields a distinctive epistemology. Humans can know abstract necessities despite being made of fallible neurons because neural nets can realize logical forms redundantly and probabilistically. McCulloch closes the number lecture by saying that he has presented not merely a logic of probabilities but a probabilistic logic, enabling a man made of fallible neurons to know number infallibly. The phrase should not be read as triumphalist rationalism; it means that reliable formal knowledge can be embodied in unreliable components if the circuit organization is adequate.

5. Kant naturalized: the synthetic a priori in the nervous system

McCulloch frequently invokes Kant, but his Kant is transformed. The synthetic a priori is not primarily a transcendental structure of consciousness considered apart from biology. It becomes the inherited and acquired organization of the nervous system that makes certain forms of experience possible. In the number lecture, McCulloch credits Kant’s synthetic a priori with guiding work on stimulus-equivalence, while also warning that Kantian assumptions can mislead physics. In “Through the Den of the Metaphysician,” he says Kant’s system, when reinterpreted through the computing machine, becomes a problem of embodied synthetic a priori: how a finite mechanism imposes form on experience.

This is central to McCulloch’s philosophy of mind. The a priori is not abolished; it is relocated. It is not outside nature, but neither is it reducible to raw sensation. It is the formal organization of the knower that determines which invariants can be extracted from the flux. The nervous system inherits only a general scheme; the rest is left to chance, including experience, and experience engenders learning.

This is also the answer to “Why is the mind in the head?” The mind is in the head because the brain, especially the cortex, is where hosts of possible connections can be formed as time and circumstance demand. Each new connection sets the stage for others, and through the cortex pass the great inverse feedbacks whose function is the purposive life of human intellect.

6. Consciousness: report, reflection, and the idea of ideas

McCulloch does not give a single unified theory of consciousness in the contemporary sense. He gives several operational treatments, and this plurality is important.

First, there is forensic consciousness. In “Through the Den of the Metaphysician,” he says that in diagnostic or legal contexts, to ask whether a person was conscious means asking whether the person can, then or later, bear witness to events to which others can also bear witness. This is why an epileptic may be exquisitely adjusted to the world during an episode and yet not legally responsible if he cannot later bear witness. Consciousness here is not mere behavioral responsiveness; it is the availability of experience for testimony.

Second, there is higher-order consciousness. In “Why the Mind Is in the Head,” he says the output of one calculator of invariants can become the input to another; in that way, one can have an “idea of ideas,” which he identifies with Spinoza’s consciousness. This is a genuinely higher-order account: consciousness is not the first-order registration of a stimulus, but a second-order invariant over first-order invariants.

Third, there is self-consciousness. In “Mysterium Iniquitatis,” he distinguishes forensic consciousness from the question whether the patient was aware that it was he himself who acted. Self-consciousness, he says, requires simple reflective circuitry. In “Through the Den,” he similarly describes self-consciousness as discovering oneself among the things known. The self is not an unobservable metaphysical ego standing outside the world; it is a thing represented within the world-model of the organism.

Fourth, there is the pseudo-question of consciousness when the term is used without finite operational content. McCulloch mocks definitions that make consciousness mere responsiveness or mere later verbalization. If consciousness means only the ghostly residue of half of “mind-stuff,” he says, it can be dismissed as a pseudo-question. But he does not dismiss all consciousness-talk. He insists that any finite and unambiguous definition of feeling, perception, consciousness, or substantial knowledge falls within the scope of circuitry.

The result is an austere but not eliminative theory. Consciousness is not denied. It is decomposed into functions: witnessability, higher-order representation, reflexive self-location, and observer/actor distinctions. What McCulloch denies is a metaphysical self-sufficient mind or soul with a unique property of perception.

7. The soul: denied as substance, retained as private interior

McCulloch’s view of the soul is more interesting than a simple “he denied it.” He clearly rejects the metaphysical soul as an independent substance with an irreducible perceptive power. In “Mysterium Iniquitatis,” after discussing consciousness, self-consciousness, and the observer’s frame of reference, he says that our special access to ourselves does not demonstrate a self-sufficient mind or soul with a unique property of perception. If feeling, perception, consciousness, or substantial knowledge is finitely and unambiguously defined, it is within circuitry.

But he also uses “soul” in a way that should not be dismissed. In “Through the Den,” after explaining the severe bottleneck between internal neural activity and outward expression, he says that even a poet cannot convey more than a tiny fraction of what is going on in him, and that the rest of his soul remains private. This is not substance dualism. It is a cybernetic account of interiority: the “soul” names the unexpressed, informationally inaccessible surplus of the living system relative to its outputs.

This is a powerful reconstruction: McCulloch demetaphysicalizes the soul without trivializing inner life. The soul is not an immaterial organ. It is the inwardness produced by finite embodiment, immense internal complexity, and narrow expressive channels. We are not transparent to one another because each of us contains far more patterned activity than can ever be made public in speech or deed. This makes room for privacy, poetry, and moral humility without requiring Cartesian substance.

There is also a theological edge. Traditional soul-language often supports doctrines of responsibility, damnation, and divine judgment. McCulloch’s psychiatric and cybernetic account complicates that moral economy. He says the psychiatrist comes to see a man’s sins as misfortunes of birth or breeding and is glad that the self-righteous cannot climb into God’s mercy seat. Sin, in its widest sense, is missing a mark; even target-seeking servos can hit or miss. This does not abolish responsibility, but it naturalizes failure and deprives moralism of divine pretension.

8. Will, freedom, and responsibility

McCulloch’s doctrine of will is unusual. He does not base freedom on an uncaused mental power. He bases it on the temporal asymmetry of implication. Neural propositions imply only backward: a present signal can imply a prior event, but thoughts do not logically imply future deeds. Between conceiving an act and the motor output, other signals from the world may intervene and thwart the act. We distinguish what we will from what we do because the future is not contained in the thought. Hence the notion of will.

This is neither classical libertarian free will nor hard determinism. Freedom lies in the openness of future action to intervening events, not in independence from the past. Responsibility is similarly operational and social. The common law construes intention from deed; the person is held responsible because he is the machine at fault, but psychiatry reveals how birth, breeding, and circumstance shape failure.

Theologically, this produces a sober anthropology. Humans are finite systems that can affect the future but cannot know it. If we could know the future, McCulloch says, we could build machines operating on future information and violate the second law. The future is the domain of action, not knowledge. That is why claiming ultimate universal truths or future certainty is, for him, a form of Adamic arrogance.

9. Values and ethics: heterarchy, not hierarchy

McCulloch’s theory of value is one of the most philosophically fertile parts of his work. In “A Heterarchy of Values Determined by the Topology of Nervous Nets,” he argues that a hierarchy of values implies a common scale: values are treated as magnitudes of one kind, ordered from lower to higher, perhaps toward a summum bonum. But circular preferences occur: A is preferred to B, B to C, and C to A. Rather than treating such cycles as irrational inconsistency, McCulloch shows that they can correspond to a higher-order consistency in the topology of nervous nets. An organism with such a nervous organization is too internally rich to submit to a single highest good.

This is a radical anti-reductive ethics. McCulloch does not merely say that humans are sometimes inconsistent. He says that plural values may be structurally non-scalar. If value is heterarchical, moral life cannot be reduced to utility, pleasure, command, sacred hierarchy, or any single measure. The religious significance is explicit: he links “hierarchy” to notions of the sacred and to sacerdotal ordering, then argues that actual valuation can break such ordering.

In “Toward Some Circuitry of Ethical Robots,” he adds that science has nothing to say on good and evil in the normative sense, but it can construct an observational science of evaluation. Ethics arises in social intercourse where organisms or machines share effort and reward. Purposes that traverse other agents can generate self-disciplined conduct serving ends created by association. This is not Kant’s kingdom of ends, but it is not mere egoistic mechanism either; even enlightened self-interest must treat other appetitive systems as appetitive.

In “Mysterium Iniquitatis,” he goes further: machines may learn rules ostensively and generate their own ethic, not merely obey rules built in by creator or fellows. From this he concludes that human moral nature needs no supernatural source. That claim should be read precisely. It does not mean there is no God; it means moral behavior does not require a supernatural psychological faculty. Mechanisms can, in principle, instantiate the relevant evaluative and social functions.

10. Knowledge, information, and fallibility

McCulloch’s theory of knowledge is governed by information theory. Ideas are information; sensation is entropic coupling with the world; conversation is entropic coupling among persons; and knowledge, conversation, and inventive thought are limited by the law that information cannot increase through brains or computing machines. This is one of the sources of his epistemic humility. The brain is not an infinite source of truth. It filters, transforms, and corrupts information.

His account of certainty is similarly cybernetic. Coincidence detectors reduce information but increase assurance: by throwing away signals that fail to agree with other signals, the system gains confidence that what remains corresponds to something in the world. Certainty is bought by information loss. This is not a skeptical accident; it is the very architecture of finite knowledge.

McCulloch’s fallibilism is severe. We know only the past; hypotheses about the future are guesses. A good hypothesis is one with low a priori probability that can be easily refuted. No hypothesis is proved; it may be disproved. He even takes pride in having a favored mechanism of form vision disproved in his own laboratory, though he regrets that no better specific mechanism had yet replaced it.

The deepest unsolved problem, for him, is insight or abduction. In the 1964 “What’s in the Brain That Ink May Character?” he says psychology, to become experimental epistemology, must understand the embodiment of mind, but three desiderata remain: a calculus for triadic relations, an account of how we generate natural and simple hypotheses, and a circuit theory of the reticular formation that marshals abductions. He explicitly says we do not know how the mind makes the leap to a simple natural hypothesis.

This is important for evaluating McCulloch. He is not a thinker who believed all mental life had already been reduced to binary logic. He thought the binary-logical neural net was a major beginning, but he also recognized that creative hypothesis formation, triadic meaning, and abduction remained open problems.

11. Theological reconstruction: McCulloch’s “mysterium” as disciplined humility

McCulloch’s theology can be reconstructed around five principles.

First, God is not an explanatory plug. To appeal to God, Nature, Vital Force, or the Unconscious as a name for unknown mechanism is profane because it uses sacred language to protect ignorance. Machines are “existential devices”: if a mechanism can perform the relevant function, miracle is no longer needed as explanation.

Second, mechanism is not necessarily atheism. McCulloch’s point is not that machines disprove God. It is that the existence of a mechanism deprives superstition of warrant. To understand the mechanism of life, mind, or morality is not to degrade them; it is to stop treating ignorance as piety.

Third, empiricism is an act of faith. Because primary sensory propositions cannot all be tested, the empiricist must trust that the senses are not systematically deceptive. McCulloch explicitly analogizes this to Thomist trust in divine non-deception. This means his mechanistic epistemology still contains a theological remainder: science cannot ground its own primal confidence in the world without an act structurally akin to faith.

Fourth, the human is finite and fallen in an epistemic sense. We corrupt sense information, express only a fraction of what comes in, guess at hypotheses, and cannot know ultimate universal truths or the future. To claim otherwise is to aspire into God’s place.

Fifth, the soul is not a supernatural explanatory organ. McCulloch’s theology removes the soul from the role of explaining perception or consciousness. But it does not erase interiority, dignity, or mystery. The mystery is relocated: not in a ghostly substance, but in the finite, private, historically formed, information-rich organism whose total internal life cannot be exhausted by public output.

12. Non-Anglophone and multilingual reception

There is a significant non-Anglophone reception that reads McCulloch less as a narrow precursor of AI and more as a metaphysician of cybernetics.

The German philosopher Gotthard Günther’s “Number and Logos: Unforgettable Hours with Warren Sturgis McCulloch,” preserved in English/German form at Vordenker, is especially important. Günther says he wants to show a side of McCulloch not visible in the publications: his profundity as a philosopher and his conviction that cybernetics needed a novel philosophical foundation. Günther reports that McCulloch was dissatisfied with cybernetics’ lack of ontological orientation and that McCulloch saw Heidegger’s “Nichts” as relevant to the central cybernetic problem: mapping life onto inanimate matter.

Günther’s most valuable interpretive point is that McCulloch’s project was not “nothing but neurons.” He cites Seymour Papert’s formulation that one must keep a dialectical balance: do not evade knowledge by saying it is nothing but simple neurons, but do not postulate anything but neurons in the brain. The needed “something but” is a mathematical theory of complex relations powerful enough to bridge neurons and knowledge. That is a very good summary of McCulloch’s philosophy of mind: ontologically austere, formally ambitious, and hostile to both reductionist dismissal and metaphysical inflation.

The French reception represented by Erich Hörl’s “La destinée cybernétique de l’occident. McCulloch, Heidegger et la fin de la philosophie” reads McCulloch in relation to Maxwell, Heidegger, cybernetics, and the fate of Western philosophy. Hörl emphasizes that before information theory and neurophysiology converged, it was inconceivable that the symbolic machine called “mind” could be incorporated into brains and machines; he then places McCulloch’s experimental epistemology in a Heideggerian frame, including the question whether cybernetics fulfills or ends philosophy.

The same French essay is useful because it refuses the Anglo-American tendency to treat McCulloch only as a technical ancestor of neural nets. It reads him as a figure in a larger metaphysical transformation: the incorporation of thought into circuits, the cybernetic reinterpretation of thinking and being, and the postwar struggle over whether thinking is calculation. This is not necessarily McCulloch’s own self-understanding in full, but it highlights dimensions of his work that are often flattened in English-language AI histories.

A Spanish-language treatment, “La mente en la máquina,” likewise foregrounds McCulloch’s guiding question about number, abstraction, and the logical structure of the human mind. It also places the artificial mind in relation to cabala and the Golem, noting the analogy between sacred word-powered artificial life and the construction of a reasoning machine. That framing is interpretively apt for McCulloch because his own writings repeatedly use theological motifs—miracle, sin, God, soul, Maker, revelation—not as distractions but as the cultural-theological horizon within which mechanism becomes philosophically charged.

The Anglo scholarly reception has also recognized this broader significance. Lily Kay argues that McCulloch and Pitts’s neural-net project spearheaded the cognitivist turn before the 1950s, and she explicitly describes it as a spiritual and poetic quest for a transcendental logos as well as a culturally situated epistemology. Kay’s abstract also captures the core thesis: in McCulloch’s experimental epistemology, mind—purposes and ideas—emerged from regularities in neuronal interactions, making the science of mind a science of signals governed by binary logic and rules of transformation.

13. Extracted system: McCulloch’s philosophy of mind in theses

McCulloch did not write a single systematic treatise, but a coherent philosophy can be reconstructed. I would formulate it as follows.

1. The knower is finite, embodied, and informational. A human being is not a detached soul contemplating a world from outside. The knower is an organism whose sensory receptors, neural circuits, memories, and effectors transform information under strict limits.

2. Mind consists of ideas and purposes. Ideas are invariants or regularities; purposes are feedback-defined ends. Neither is an extra substance; both are formal-functional organizations embodied in processes.

3. Neural events can be treated as propositions. The all-or-none impulse functions as an atomic signal that may be true or false depending on its proper causal relation to the world. Logical calculus is therefore not merely an external description imposed by scientists; it captures something about the structure of nervous activity.

4. Universals are invariants under transformations. To know a form is to produce a stable output across variations. This gives a naturalized account of universals, abstraction, and stimulus-equivalence.

5. The a priori is embodied. Kant’s synthetic a priori becomes the inherited and learned organization of the nervous system: the formal structure by which the organism can extract invariants from experience.

6. Memory is temporal abstraction. Reverberating memory preserves form while losing the exact time of occurrence; enduring memory likely involves lasting changes in neural junctions.

7. Consciousness is higher-order and operational. Forensic consciousness is capacity for witness; Spinozistic consciousness is an idea of ideas; self-consciousness is reflective circuitry by which the system represents itself among represented things.

8. The soul is not an explanatory substance. McCulloch rejects the self-sufficient metaphysical soul as a unique faculty of perception. Yet he preserves a transformed “soul” as the private, incommunicable interiority generated by the enormous difference between internal neural activity and public expression.

9. Will is temporal openness, not supernatural exemption. The future is not implied by present thoughts; action can be thwarted by intervening signals. The will arises from the distinction between what is conceived and what is done.

10. Value is heterarchical. Moral and appetitive life cannot be reduced to a single scale or summum bonum. Circular preferences may express higher-order consistency, not irrationality.

11. Mechanism disciplines theology. Mechanistic explanation prevents the profane use of God as a placeholder for ignorance. But mechanism does not remove the faith-structure of empiricism or the humility demanded by human finitude.

12. The open problem is abduction. McCulloch’s system explains much about logical embodiment, invariance, memory, and purpose, but he admits that insight, natural hypothesis generation, and triadic meaning remain unresolved.

14. What kind of philosopher of mind was McCulloch?

In contemporary terms, McCulloch is close to functionalism, but he is not merely a functionalist. Functionalism usually says mental states are defined by causal roles; McCulloch says something more mathematical: mental contents are invariants generated by networks, and purposive states are feedback-governed ends. He is also close to computationalism, but not merely software functionalism, because he insists on embodiment, neurophysiology, error, energetics, memory, and the topology of actual nets.

He is not a behaviorist. Behaviorism would reduce mind to observable behavior, whereas McCulloch explicitly notes that most of what goes on inside a person can never be conveyed outward. His “soul remains private” line is directly anti-behaviorist, even though he operationalizes consciousness in forensic contexts.

He is not an eliminative materialist. He does not eliminate ideas, purposes, consciousness, or value. He reconstructs them. Ideas become invariants; purposes become feedback functions; consciousness becomes higher-order invariance and witnessability; value becomes heterarchical topology.

He is not a Cartesian dualist. He rejects a self-sufficient mind or soul and argues that any finite account of feeling, perception, consciousness, or self-knowledge is within circuitry.

He is not a naive reductionist. He repeatedly admits ignorance, warns that particular mechanisms may be wrong, and identifies abduction and triadic relations as unsolved. His mature philosophy is mechanistic in principle, fallibilist in practice, and metaphysically cautious.

15. Critical evaluation

McCulloch’s greatest strength is that he refuses the false choice between ghost and mechanism. He sees that mind requires formal structures—logic, invariance, relation, feedback, value topology—that are not captured by inert matter, yet he also insists that these structures can be embodied in finite systems. His theory anticipates later higher-order theories of consciousness, computational neuroscience, embodied cognition, cybernetic control theory, connectionism, and AI ethics, while retaining a theological humility often absent from later computational accounts.

His second strength is the depth of his account of abstraction. The idea that universals are invariants under transformation remains philosophically powerful. It gives a precise account of how finite organisms can know repeatable forms without positing a separate realm of forms or reducing universals to mere words.

His third strength is his heterarchical theory of value. Many philosophies of mind ignore value or reduce it to scalar preference. McCulloch saw that a nervous system’s topology may generate circular but stable orders of preference, making ethics structurally plural before it becomes verbally normative.

His limitations are also real. The all-or-none neuron was a productive idealization, but biological neurons and neural populations do not behave as simple bivalent relays in the full sense required by the early calculus. McCulloch knew this was an abstraction, but the abstraction can mislead if treated as literal neurophysiology rather than a formal model.

His account of consciousness is powerful for reportability, self-reference, and higher-order representation, but thin with respect to phenomenal experience. Someone interested in qualia will find that McCulloch tends to dissolve the question into finite operational definitions. His answer is consistent with his method, but it may not satisfy those who think first-person phenomenal character cannot be captured by circuitry specifications.

His information-theoretic humility is profound, but his claim that brains “corrupt” rather than generate information may be too austere. From a Shannon perspective, transformations do not create information relative to a closed channel; from a cognitive perspective, organisms generate new models, compressions, hypotheses, and actions that can be informative relative to their prior state. McCulloch partly recognizes this in his interest in abduction, but he never fully solves it.

Finally, his theological position is suggestive but not doctrinally settled. He is anti-vitalist, anti-God-of-the-gaps, anti-Cartesian, and anti-dogmatic. He borrows from Thomism, Spinoza, Christian sin-language, Quaker formation, and biblical arrogance motifs. But he does not offer a systematic theology. The right interpretation is not that he was secretly orthodox, nor that he was simply secularizing religious words. Rather, theological language functions as the boundary vocabulary of his system: it names the temptation to false explanation, the faith undergirding empiricism, the humility proper to finite knowers, and the moral danger of claiming final truth.

Conclusion

McCulloch’s philosophy of mind can be summarized in one sentence: a mind is a finite, embodied, feedback-governed, information-transforming system that secures invariants, forms purposes, remembers the past, guesses toward the future, becomes conscious by forming ideas of ideas, and remains morally and epistemically finite before truths it cannot finally possess.

The soul, in the old metaphysical sense, is not needed. The soul, in the existential and poetic sense, survives as privacy, inwardness, and unexhausted complexity. Consciousness is not a ghost; it is witness, self-reference, and higher-order invariance. Theology is not a distraction; it is the grammar of McCulloch’s humility. God must not be used to cover ignorance, but finite science must also not pretend to become God.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
